# **RCA: EMR Hosts out-of-date libs Security Tickets**

Status: *Under review*  Author: [Yi Chen](mailto:yichen@stripe.com)    Created: May 18, 2025  Last Updated: May 27, 2025

| May 27, 2025 | Shared this doc with \#team-data-infra for team review ([thread](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/CJFHE7AR3/p1748368305026389)) |
| :---- | :---- |
| May 27, 2025 | Merged a PR to remove the daily cron job ([thread](https://slack-pde.slack.com/archives/C07RMV6BDNJ/p1748367493804609)) |

# Executive Summary

* **Issue**: EMR hosts repeatedly trigger Nessus security tickets due to outdated libraries.  
* **Root Cause**: rpmdb corruption on EMR nodes, caused by concurrent `yum` executions from overlapping Chef runs.  
* **Evidence**: Empirical data shows repeated library update failures on the same hosts across multiple tickets.  
* **Impact**: Security tickets persist despite cluster reprovisioning; manual remediation is ineffective.  
* **Proposed Mitigation**: Add locking mechanisms to Chef executions, remove hacky daily cron job, and eliminate unsafe concurrency patterns.

# Summary

Recurring EMR security tickets—primarily those Nessus tickets linked to out-of-date libraries on EMR hosts—are caused by widespread **rpmdb corruption risk**[\[6\]](#bookmark=id.ymsmdfcv7sm). These security alerts persist even after clusters are reprovisioned. Standard mitigation (manual patching, cluster rotation) is a whack-a-mole effort, which is ineffective—problems recur. This corruption stems from concurrency risks in our Chef automation, particularly during bootstrap[\[7\]](#bookmark=id.tuonhvru9nkj) and routine runs. Unless addressed, this issue will continue to generate significant toil[\[5\]](#bookmark=id.y0n53vrrsgzi) and reinforce perceptions that EMR is unreliable and costly to maintain.

This document outlines empirical evidence supporting this root cause, details the concurrency risks in Chef usage, and proposes high-priority mitigations.

# Analysis

## Correlation between rpmdb corruption and security tickets for EMR hosts

Beginning in October 2024 ([Reducing Unnecessary Security Toil ](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1TXqazw5qBeqyL_DYNf1w7XfG84s52MzLNdcg-TmR-pY/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.lnqfcyy21mit)), a focused root cause investigation culminated in a March 2025 breakthrough: nodes flagged by security tickets frequently had corrupted rpmdb files, rendering them unable to apply updates via `yum` (see [Security Toil Investigation: CVE-2024-7264](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1BS9zzmDZzUlPao7UydR22n3LvDrXWoVHSOXjXCmsoos/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.lnqfcyy21mit)). Further analysis revealed that different tickets involving unrelated libraries often implicated the same set of hosts within narrow timeframes[\[1\]](#bookmark=id.tp4u622bgh0s)—strongly indicating a shared underlying failure.

## Chef Implementation Enable Unsafe Concurrent yum Runs

We use `chef-solo` to bootstrap and manage EMR nodes. At bootstrap, two cron jobs are installed[\[2\]](#bookmark=id.s8t7h78l9lj2): one hourly and one daily. These jobs often collide[\[5\]](#bookmark=id.y0n53vrrsgzi):

* **Bootstrap vulnerability**: A long-running initial Chef run can overlap with the scheduled cron runs. We use the same AMI for all new nodes, so the bootstrap time will get longer and longer with more and more updates needed to go through. The longer duration increases the rpmdb corruption risk[\[5\]](#bookmark=id.y0n53vrrsgzi).  
* **Concurrency risk**: The `emr::yum` recipe triggers a `yum --security upgrade -y` command if `node['emr']['first_run']` is true. However, this marker is only set at the *end* of the Chef run, allowing multiple overlapping processes to all interpret the run as the first.  
* **Persistent issue**: Even post-bootstrap, concurrent hourly/daily jobs[\[2\]](#bookmark=id.s8t7h78l9lj2) continue to risk corruption, especially during larger upgrades.

## Lack of Concurrency Controls in [run-chef.sh](http://run-chef.sh)

The `run-chef.sh` script lacks basic concurrency protections:

* No file locking for `chef-solo` calls ([L71](http://run-chef.sh#L71))  
* Log rotation is not designed for concurrent access ([L59](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/blob/master/emradm/bootstrap/setup_chef/run-chef.sh#L59))  
* `aws s3 sync` to the same folder is unsafe ([L51](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/blob/master/emradm/bootstrap/setup_chef/run-chef.sh#L51))  
* `first_run` marker written only at end of bootstrap.sh ([L135](https://slack-github.com/slack/ops-go/blob/master/emradm/bootstrap/setup_chef/bootstrap.sh#L135))

Given 200 machines, even a 1-minute overlap risk translates to \~3 hosts per day experiencing corruption—consistent with ticket patterns observed.

## Lack of Concurrency Controls in the use of Chef package blocks

Even beyond explicit `yum` calls, Chef’s `package` blocks are not thread-safe and rely on `yum` or `rpm`. These are used widely across our recipes[\[3\]](#bookmark=id.o4plyvr6l2vt) and cron-executed scripts (e.g., `hdfs_cache_cron.sh` every 15 minutes), increasing collision risk.

## Additional Risk: Ad Hoc Cron Job Hacks

Screenshots show that in addition to standard cron jobs, a 3-minute Nginx monitoring job also invokes `run-chef` under certain conditions. These overlapping, uncoordinated triggers dramatically increase rpmdb corruption risk.

![][image1]

## Root Cause Confirmation

To confirm that rpmdb corruption is the systemic root cause, I examined:

* Batch nature of security ticket detection  
* Repeated flagging of same hosts across different vulnerabilities  
* Persistence of issues post-reprovisioning

This pattern aligns with what we’d expect if affected nodes are unable to upgrade *any* packages after corruption, not just those in a specific CVE.

# Corrective Actions

## \[High\] Eliminate unnecessary daily chef cron

Review and remove the daily cron jobs if the root cause of its existence is unknown.

## \[High\] Protect chef-solo with a lock

Use `flock` to prevent concurrent execution:

```
flock /var/run/chef.lock chef-solo --run-lock-timeout="90" -o emr::default --chef-license accept -c /mnt/chef/solo.rb 2>&1 | tee "$CHEF_RUN_LOG_FILE"
```

## \[High\] Protect run-chef calls with flock

We can either protect the run-chef from the caller in the cron job. Or modify the run-chef script from inside to ensure only one is in the running state.

Example of first option:

```
# Hourly cron
0 * * * * /usr/bin/flock -n /var/run/run-chef.lock /usr/local/bin/run-chef.sh --chef-env=prod ...
# Daily cron
30 1 * * * /usr/bin/flock -n /var/run/run-chef.lock /usr/local/bin/run-chef.sh --chef-env=prod ...
```

Example of second option, which ensures that run-chef is always safe no matter where it is being called from

```
#!/bin/bash
exec /usr/bin/flock -n /var/run/run-chef.lock "$0" "$@"
```

## 

## \[Medium\] Fix the root-cause of “Ensure Slack nginx dominance” Hack

Remove, or fix the root cause of this [hack solution](#bookmark=id.5uabr32mizef), which can be risky.

## \[Low\] Audit and guard all yum and package calls

Ensure each is concurrency-safe, especially in high-frequency scripts.

# Appendix

## \[1\] Empirical Pattern of Affected Hosts

If rpmdb corruption is indeed the root cause of certain Nessus tickets—such as those reporting outdated libraries like libcurl—then it’s reasonable to assume that affected machines may also fail to upgrade other libraries, potentially triggering additional Nessus tickets involving seemingly unrelated packages. We also need to account for the fact that IPv4-based hostnames may be reused after a cluster is rotated, making the vulnerability detection date a critical factor in analysis.

The following screenshot shows that at one point, a host named “ip-10-18-137-73” was detected with an outdated library. A search in JIRA surfaced five related Nessus tickets, three of which were reported within a short time frame between November 5 and 11, 2024\.

![][image2]

The following example shows another host, “ip-10-61-40-113”, which generated two tickets—one for a high-severity vulnerability and one for a low-severity vulnerability—based on Nessus detection dates that are four days apart.

![][image3]

## \[2\] Uncoordinated Cron jobs without concurrency controls

In our implementation, we have two cron jobs: one hourly and one daily. This creates a significant risk of corrupting the RPM database because neither the run-chef script nor its downstream calls are concurrency-safe. For example, run-chef invokes chef-solo, which performs package operations that are not safe for concurrent execution.

The following screenshot shows the two cron jobs. Note that the inline comment shows the daily cron job is a monkey patching, a hack, without understanding the root-cause. 

![][image4]

## \[3\] Chef “package” Blocks Trigger yum Internally

The “package” block of Chef interacts with the underlying package manager (like yum) to perform specific actions such as installing, upgrading, or removing packages, depending on the action specified in the recipe. 

The default action of package install in chef, will call “yum install” if the package is not already installed. The upgrade package action will call “yum update” to upgrade the package if a new version is detected. Neither of these actions are thread safe, so there is a good chance to corrupt the rpmdb when two chef-solo is called at the same time. 

![][image5]

## \[4\] Duration of Chef Runs

The following log (last-run.log) shows a recent chef run, which says 42/9497 resources were updated in 2 minutes. This is a recent hourly run. The first run will take a much longer time when a significantly larger number of resources need to be updated.

![][image6]

The following is the chef run duration on the same host in 5 consecutive hourly runs around the hour of (2025-05-21T16:57:59+00:00). Note that with the 5 consecutive hourly runs, the run duration is extremely consistent, and the number of resources updated is also consistent. I am not sure if this is of concern, as it seems these chef-runs appears to be repetitive instead of incremental based on these logs.

Infra Phase complete, 42/9497 resources updated in 01 minutes 56 seconds  
Infra Phase complete, 42/9497 resources updated in 01 minutes 56 seconds  
Infra Phase complete, 42/9497 resources updated in 01 minutes 55 seconds  
Infra Phase complete, 45/9497 resources updated in 01 minutes 55 seconds  
Infra Phase complete, 45/9497 resources updated in 01 minutes 56 seconds

## \[5\] Estimated Daily Collision Rate

Since the hourly runs of chef-solo takes 2 minutes[\[4\]](#bookmark=id.i010m6e6mbiz), this means that on each machine, there is a 1/30 chance to collide with the daily run each day. 

Assuming we have 200 machines in our infrastructure, and each machine has a 1/30 chance to collide and result in a corrupted rpmdb, we would expect 6.6 machines on each day that will have a collided chef run. Each such collision has a good chance of corrupting the rpmdb because of how wide-spread the risk is once there is a collision[\[6\]](#bookmark=id.ymsmdfcv7sm). If half of such collisions result in a corrupted rpmdb, we expect to see 3 hosts a day being affected.

For security tickets, usually it may detect a large number of affected machines initially, and later it will detect newly affected machines. The initial number appears to be usually bigger, and the incremental detected machines are usually in the ballpark of 3 machines, matching our calculation above.

One possible reason to explain why the initial detection number can be higher. This is because a security scanner were first deployed within Slack usually within days when a CVE is announced, no machines have the upgrades, so a larger number of machines needs the upgrade, so the initial detection may not caused by rpmdb corruption, it’s caused by the legitimate reason that a new CVE needs to be applied. The subsequent detection are most likely caused by corrupted rpmdb.

## \[6\] Wide-spread risks of corrupted rpmdb from concurrent runs of Chef recipe

It turns out that this is a very wide-spread risk, such that our whole chef runs are unsafe. Essentially, every time we use “package” in the Chef, it is running yum or rpm in the background, and each of these will create the same rpmdb corruption issue. 

The following example highlights the widespread use of package blocks in our Chef recipes. As a result, we are exposed to the risk of rpmdb corruption every day when these recipes are executed.

![][image7]

For example, the following block in hdfs.rb ([source code](https://slack-github.com/slack/chef-repo/blob/master/emr/cookbooks/emr/recipes/hdfs.rb#L30)) will execute every 15 minutes. If the \`hdfs\_cache\_cron.sh\` is not concurrency-safe, and a previous run is still executing when the next starts, we will hit many issues including file lock contention, duplicate HDFS caching attempts etc.  
![][image8]

Another example is the following code. We know that two process can run at the same time and both processes think its the first run, the following command will be executed at the same time, and if it is not thread-safe, it can create unpredictable state.

![][image9]

## \[7\] Bootstrap Risk

All of our EC2 nodes are based on the same AMI, so it will go through the same process and take about the same amount of time during bootstrap. 

We use chef-solo to provision EMR clusters. At cluster bootstrap time, our bootstrap script will install the Chef recipes, and also set up a cron job to run Chef on a regular basis. As a matter of fact, there are two scheduled chef runs, one hourly job ([source code](https://slack-github.com/slack/chef-repo/blob/master/emr/cookbooks/emr/recipes/default.rb#L15)), and one daily job ([source code](https://slack-github.com/slack/chef-repo/blob/master/emr/cookbooks/emr/recipes/default.rb#L24)). The daily job appears to be a hack for an issue without a root-cause.

Because these two cron jobs can run concurrently, there’s also a risk of collision during bootstrap. The following screenshot shows the first statement in yum.rb, which can be triggered simultaneously at bootstrap time. This means the highlighted statement may be executed concurrently — a known risk factor for rpmdb corruption.

![][image10]

The statement above may appear safe, as it is conditioned to run only during the first Chef execution. However, this condition is not reliable. The first\_run marker is set only after the entire Chef run completes successfully ([source code](https://slack-github.com/slack/chef-repo/blob/master/emr/cookbooks/emr/recipes/default.rb#L168)). Since both hourly and daily scheduled Chef runs can be triggered concurrently—especially during the first run—it’s possible for two Chef processes to interpret the run as the “first” before the marker file is written. As a result, they may simultaneously execute the yum \--security upgrade \-y command, leading to rpmdb corruption early in the node’s lifecycle.
